coredata.dc:title: Conductive Co3O4/graphene (core/shell) quantum dots as electrode materials for electrochemical pseudocapacitor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Shim, Jaeho
Ko, Yohan
Lee, Kyu Seung
Partha, Khanra
Lee, Chil-Hyoung
Yu, Kyoungsik
Koo, Hye Young
Lee, Ki-Tae
Seo, Won-Seon
Son, Dong Ick
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the in-situ chemical synthesis and their properties of the core (active material)/shell (conductive material) type Co3O4/graphene quantum dots (QDs). The stable oxygen bridge bonds between the Co3O4 core and the oxygen-related functional groups on the graphene shells facilitate the efficient charge/discharge performance. The efficient electron transfer process between the Co3O4 core and the graphene shell lead to an improvement in the electrochemical activity. The specific capacitances of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode, without conductive additives, exhibit high values of 2435 F g−1 at current densities of 1 A g−1. It breaks through 126% of the initial capacitance after 500 cycles and show above 112% even after 4500 cycles. The excellent performance of the Co3O4/G QDs electrode is attributed to the significant improvement of the electrochemical activity, without conductive additives, due to the presence of metal oxide QD covered by graphene shells which leads to the good electrical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid
Nano-structures
Chemical analysis
Electron microscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication methods of the Co3O4/graphene electrodes
Fabrication methods of the Co3O4 electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of chemical exfoliation of graphene sheets from chemical bonding between graphite oxide and Co3O4 QDs. (b) Schematic of synthesis of Co3O4/graphene QDs by the reaction between graphene sheets and surface of Co3O4 QDs. (c) The QDs are synthesized via chemical reaction between three kinds of functional groups (carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH) and epoxy) of GO and Co3+. The magnified image shows chemical bonding between the functional groups and Co3+. (d) Schematic of core/shell type structured Co3O4/graphene QDs with Co3O4 QDs covered by graphene. (e) TEM image of Co3O4/graphene QDs. An enlarged view of the area marked by the red dotted square shows well-crystallized nanocrystals with lattice interplanar spacing corresponding to the (220) facets of Co3O4. (f) TEM images of graphene shells. An enlarged view of the area marked by the red dotted square shows hexagonal structure with side length 0.14 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of Co3O4/graphene QDs. The (002) peaks of graphene and (220), (311), and (400) peaks of Co3O4 in the X-ray diffraction patterns provide evidence that the Co3O4/graphene QDs contain graphene and Co3O4 quantum dots. (b) Raman spectra of Co3O4/graphene QDs. (c) High resolution C1 XPS spectra of the Co3O4/graphene QDs. (d) High resolution Co2p XPS spectra of the Co3O4/graphene QDs.

(a) Schematic of three-electrode system. (b) Cyclic voltammograms of the Bare Co3O4 electrode and Co3O4/graphene electrode at 40 mV s−1 scan rate. (c) Discharge profiles of the Bare Co3O4 electrode and Co3O4/graphene electrode at a constant current density of 1 A g−1. (d) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the Bare Co3O4 electrode and Co3O4/graphene electrode.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of the Co3O4/graphene electrode at various scan rates (10, 20, 40, and 80 mV s−1). (b) The cycle performance of the Co3O4/graphene electrode in the potential window between 0 and 0.5 V at 2 A g−1. The inset shows the discharge profiles of the Co3O4/graphene electrode at various current density (1, 2, 4 and 8 A g−1).

head.ce:title: Conductive Co3O4/graphene (core/shell) quantum dots as electrode materials for electrochemical pseudocapacitor applications

Korea University of Science and Technology (UST)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), Jeonbuk 565-905, Republic of Korea. Tel.: +82 63 2198155; fax: +82 63 2198129.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the in-situ chemical synthesis and their properties of the core (active material)/shell (conductive material) type Co3O4/graphene quantum dots (QDs). The stable oxygen bridge bonds between the Co3O4 core and the oxygen-related functional groups on the graphene shells facilitate the efficient charge/discharge performance. The efficient electron transfer process between the Co3O4 core and the graphene shell lead to an improvement in the electrochemical activity. The specific capacitances of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode, without conductive additives, exhibit high values of 2435 F g−1 at current densities of 1 A g−1. It breaks through 126% of the initial capacitance after 500 cycles and show above 112% even after 4500 cycles. The excellent performance of the Co3O4/G QDs electrode is attributed to the significant improvement of the electrochemical activity, without conductive additives, due to the presence of metal oxide QD covered by graphene shells which leads to the good electrical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: With increasing energy demands required to sustain current living standards, there is a need for the development of high-performance energy storage systems. The batteries and the electrochemical capacitors are energy storage system which use the chemical reaction for energy storage. Unlike the batteries, the electrochemical capacitors use the charge/discharge phenomena which caused by ion transfer between working electrode and electrolyte or surface chemistry. Therefore, electrochemical capacitors have long cycle life and safety tolerance to high-rate charge and discharge [1]. In addition, the electrochemical capacitors have higher power density than secondary batteries and higher energy density than conventional capacitors. Among the electrochemical capacitors, pseudocapacitors use fast and reversible faradaic reactions on the surface. For improving electrochemical capacitor's properties, pseudocapacitive transition metal oxides such as MnO2, RuO2, NiO and Co3O4 and their composites with conductive additives which could produce higher specific capacitance than typical carbonaceous materials have been investigated [2–8].

Among pseudocapacitive transition metal oxides, Co3O4 is a low cost material, environmental benign, exhibit great redox activity and has high theoretical specific capacitance (3560 F g−1) [9,10]. However, it is a great challenge to improve the observed specific capacitance of Co3O4 as well as to achieve high reversible theoretical specific capacitance with good rate capability and long term stability.

In pseudocapacitors, the carbon black is generally used to increase conductivity of the electrodes as the typical active materials of the pseudocapacitors have low conductivity. However, the carbon black has lower specific capacitance compared to that of Co3O4. Therefore, as an alternative to the carbon black, the conductive Co3O4 based material can be used as a high performance electrode for pseudocapacitors without compromising on the conductivity.

Some studies concerning the combination of CO3O4 nanostructures with graphene have been performed [11,12]. However, these studies use the graphene as a support for anchoring nanostructures and their electrode needs the conductive additives.

In this study, we designed core (active material)/shell (conductive material) type Co3O4/graphene quantum dots (Co3O4/graphene QDs) as electrode materials for high performance electrochemical energy storages without conductive additives. The Co3O4/graphene QDs were synthesized by a simple and facile technique. In situ growth of the Co3O4/graphene QDs was achieved using a solution-process method consisting of conjugation between functionalized graphene made by acid treatments of graphite and the in-situ grown embryo Co3O4 QDs. We also investigated the structural, chemical and morphological properties of the Co3O4/graphene QDs by various techniques, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The electrochemical performances were investigated by cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic cycling.

The graphite powder (Aldrich, 5 g) with 120 mL of 1:3 mixture of HNO3 (17 M) and H2SO4 (18 M) aqueous solutions were ultrasonicated for 2 h, while maintaining the temperature at 45 °C to make the GO powders. Thereafter, the solution of cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Aldrich, 18.4 g) with GO powders (400 mg) in dimethylformamide (DMF) (2000 mL) was heated to 120 °C and maintained at that temperature for 5 h to make the Co3O4/graphene QDs.
The solution of cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Aldrich, 18.4 g) in dimethylformamide (DMF) (2000 mL) was heated to 120 °C and maintained at that temperature for 5 h to make the Co3O4 QDs.

The Co3O4/graphene QDs were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectra, transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The XRD patterns were obtained by a Panalytical Empyrean X-ray Diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The TEM images were obtained by a JEOL JEM-3010 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 200 kV. Raman spectra for the Co3O4/graphene QDs were measured at room temperature by spreading the synthesized materials on a glass substrate and using Horiba Jobin Yvon-Labram HR UV–Visible–NIR Raman microscope spectrometer with 514 nm argon laser excitation on a 600 lines/mm grating. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted with a ULVAC PHI5000 VERSA PROBE system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication methods of the Co3O4/graphene electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The Co3O4/graphene QDs and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a weight ratio of 95:5 were dispersed in an N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution to make a slurry. The slurry was coated on a piece of the Ni foam substrate and dried in a vacuum oven at 120 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication methods of the Co3O4 electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The Co3O4 QDs, the acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a weight ratio of 85:10:5 were dispersed in an N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution to make a slurry. The slurry was coated on a piece of the Ni foam substrate and dried in a vacuum oven at 120 °C for 12 h.
The electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode system with a platinum foil as counter electrode and an alkaline Hg/HgO reference electrode in a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (ZIVE SP1, Wonatech, Inc.) at various scan rates in the voltage range of 0–0.6 V. The constant current charge-discharge was tested at 1 A g−1, 2 A g−1, 4 A g−1, 8 A g−1 current densities.
EIS was carried out in the frequency range from 0.01 to 1 MHz at open circuit potential with an amplitude of 5 mV.

The chemical synthesis process of Co3O4/graphene QDs is graphically summarized in Fig. 1 a–d. First, the graphite oxide (GO) powder (Fig. 1a) was prepared by the acid treatment of graphite powders with a mixed acid of H2SO4 and HNO3. During the process, the graphite powder gets converted into graphene pieces, having three functional groups of carboxyl (−COOH), hydroxyl (−OH), and epoxy (C−O−C). Thereafter, the solution of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate with GO powder in DMF was heated to make the Co3O4/graphene QDs.

The embryo Co3O4 QDs, appearing with cube like structure, were preferentially formed in the early stages under the chemical reaction 3Co(CH3COO)2⋅4H2O + 12.5O2 → Co3O4 + 12CO2 + 21H2O and grew up to the size about 7 nm in diameter. Then, formation of interfacial Co−O−C bonds were most likely occurred at the outermost edge of the embryo Co3O4 QDs [13–18]. During this reaction, the graphene encircle to the embryo Co3O4 QDs as core/shell structure Co3O4/graphene QDs, as shown in the TEM images of Fig. 1d–e.

The definitive evidence of structural properties of the Co3O4/graphene QDs were obtained through TEM, XRD, Raman spectra and XPS. For a thorough investigation of the structure of the outer layers covering the inner Co3O4 QDs, the Co3O4/graphene core/shell QDs were analyzed using TEM analysis. Plane-view bright-field TEM image of the Co3O4/graphene QDs is shown in Fig. 1d. The TEM image indicates that the Co3O4/graphene QDs are well formed with diameters of about 7 nm. An enlarged view of the TEM image reveals that the interlayer spacing in the crystalline petal is 0.28 nm, which corresponds to the interplanar distance of the (220) crystal planes of cubic Co3O4 [19]. Fig. 1f clearly indicates that the Co3O4 core is covered by the graphene shells. The high resolution TEM image of the Co3O4/graphene QDs (Fig. 1f) shows the lattice structure of graphene.

As seen from the XRD patterns of Co3O4/graphene QDs (Fig. 2 a), simultaneous observation of Bragg peaks corresponding to the Co3O4 and the graphene indicates that the Co3O4/graphene QDs were successfully synthesized. The existence of the graphene on the Co3O4 QDs is confirmed by the appearance of both a broad intense peak of G (002) centered at around 2θ = 25.8° (d = 0.34 nm) with a large full width at half maximum (HWFM) as much as 2.34°. For Co3O4, the Bragg peaks (220), (311) and (400) were observed and the position and intensity ratios of their peaks agree well with those of standard Co3O4 bulk peaks (JCPDS No. 78–1969).

Fig. 2b presents the Raman spectra of the Co3O4/graphene QDs. The peaks at 480, 525, 615 and 681 cm−1 corresponds to Eg, F2 2g, F3 2g and A1g modes of Co3O4 [20]. The G and D modes are known to arise from first order scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms and from a breathing mode of k-point photons of A1g symmetry, confirming the graphene shell structure [21].

The C1s XPS spectrum of Co3O4/graphene QDs (Fig. 2c) clearly indicates a considerable degree of oxidation with five different kinds of carbon atoms. The peaks located at 284.3, 285.5, 286.4, 287.5 and 288.7 eV correspond to different functional groups of carbon: C=C, C−OH (hydroxyls), C−O−C (epoxy/ether), C=O (carbonyls), COOH (acids) groups, respectively. This also indicates that the rich oxygen groups are contained within graphene.

The surface properties and elemental valences of Co3O4/graphene QDs were further studied by XPS. The core level Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 in the Co3O4/graphene QDs was deconvoluted to various fitted peaks (Fig. 2d). The peaks at binding energies of 780.1, 786.8 and 795.7 eV can be assigned to Co3+ and the other peaks at a higher binding energies (782.2, 789.4 and 798.0 eV) can be assigned to Co2+ [22,23]. The BE value with a spin–orbit splitting of ca. 15.5 eV (795.6 eV in the Co2p1/2 region to 780.1 eV in the Co2p3/2 region) indicates that the Co3+ species are predominant on the surface [23–25].

We measured the electrochemical performance of the Co3O4/graphene and Co3O4 electrodes. The Co3O4/graphene electrode was made up of Co3O4/graphene QDs supported on Ni Foam without carbon black. On the other hand, the Co3O4 electrode was made up of Co3O4 QDs supported on Ni Foam including carbon black.

The electrochemical tests were carried out in a three-electrode system with a platinum foil as the counter electrode and an alkaline reference electrode (Hg/HgO) in a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte (Fig. 3 a).

Fig. 3b shows the cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of the Co3O4 and Co3O4/graphene electrodes at a scan rate of 40 mV s−1. For both the electrodes, a couple of well-defined redox peaks within the potential range from 0 to 0.6 V are clearly observed on the CV curves. Two strong redox peaks were observed evidently in the CV curves [26]. There are two typical redox couples of Co3O4, which correspond to the two sequential reactions described as follows:
Co3O4 + OH− + H2O ↔ 3CoOOH + e−
CoOOH + OH− ↔ CoO2 + H2O + e−

Compared with Co3O4 electrodes, the Co3O4/graphene electrode has larger CV area, indicating the higher levels of stored charge due to the thin graphene shell which enhances the electrical conductivity, avoids aggregation, improves chemical stability, and buffers the stress of the inner nanoscale active material [27].

Fig. 3c shows the discharge curves of the Co3O4/graphene and Co3O4 electrodes obtained within the potential range 0 and 0.5 V at a constant current density of 1 A g−1. The profiles of each curve have a discharge voltage plateau in the discharge process, and these plateaus are matched with the reduction peaks observed in the CV curves, respectively. This phenomenon indicates that the samples exhibit pseudocapacitance properties. However, the length and height of the discharge plateau are different for Co3O4/graphene and Co3O4 electrodes. From the discharge curves, the specific capacitance can be calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
C =
I Δ t
m Δ V
where C (F g−1) is the specific capacitance, I represent the discharge current, Δt is the discharging time, ΔV is the voltage window, and m is the mass of materials. The specific capacitances of Co3O4 and Co3O4/graphene electrodes are 1457 and 2435 F g−1, respectively. As expected, the specific capacitance of the Co3O4/graphene electrode is higher than that of the Co3O4 electrode, indicating that the Co3O4/graphene electrode has a better discharge capacity during the charge–discharge process. The result is in good agreement with the survey from CV curves. Furthermore, graphene on the surface helps the diffusion of electrolyte into the inner region of the electrode and hence enhances the mass transport of the electrolyte within the electrode for fast redox reaction. This results in the reduced diffusion resistance, high utilization percent of electroactive materials and enhanced diffusion kinetics.

Fig. 3d shows the electrochemical process probed by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurement. EIS was carried out in the frequency range from 0.01 to 1 MHz at open circuit potential with an amplitude of 5 mV, where Z′ and Z″ are the real and imaginary parts of the impedance, respectively. The Nyquist plot of the Co3O4 and the Co3O4/graphene electrode displays a linear part in the low frequency region and a semicircle in the high frequency region. The high frequency range is the resistance resulting from the frequency dependence of ion diffusion/transport. The low resistance of ion transfer in the Co3O4/graphene electrode may contribute to the high capacitance.

The detailed electrochemical performance of Co3O4/graphene electrodes are further investigated as shown in Fig. 4 . The CV curves of Co3O4/graphene within the potential window of 0–0.6 V (vs Hg/HgO) at different scan rates of 10, 20, and 40 mVs−1. At high scanning rates, the shape of the CV is changed. The anodic and cathodic peaks shift to positive and negative directions, respectively, due to the limited diffusion time and two redox couples combine into one [28].

The inset in Fig. 4b presents the discharge curves profiles of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode obtained within the potential range 0 and 0.5 V at different current densities. The specific capacitances of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode appear to have high specific capacitance values of 2435, 1782, 1471 and 1274 F g−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 4 and 8 A g−1, respectively.

The long-term specific capacitance retention of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode at a current density of 2 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 4b. The specific capacitance gradually increases at the beginning of cycles, and then there is a slight decrease, which indicates that there is an initial activation process for faradaic pseudo capacitance of Co3O4. To be surprised, the capacitance of Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode does not degrade but increase continuously between 1000 cycle to 3000 cycle. Eventually it reaches to 126% of the initial capacitance after 500 cycles and exhibit above 112% even after 4500 cycles.

In summary, Co3O4/graphene QDs are successfully synthesized using simple solution method. TEM, XRD, Raman spectra and XPS showed that core-shell structured Co3O4/graphene QDs are well formed by a simple and facile chemical reaction. The stable oxygen bridge bonds between the core Co3O4 QDs and the oxygen-related functional groups on the graphene shell enhanced the conductivity of the Co3O4/graphene QDs. When evaluated as electrode material for electrochemical capacitors, the Co3O4/graphene QDs exhibit high specific capacitance and superior rate capability. The remarkable electrochemical performance could be ascribed to the unique core-shell structures that facilitate the electrolyte penetration and provide more reactive sites for faradaic reactions. Therefore, the excellent performance of the Co3O4/graphene QDs electrode can be explained that graphene shells provide the good electrical properties without the conductive additives for electrodes. The Co3O4 (active material)/graphene (conductive material) QDs which can make the electrode without conductive additives is believed to be promising electrode material for high performance electrochemical pseudocapacitor applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: D. I. Son appreciates the financial support from the KIST Institution Program. Also, this research was supported by Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2016M3A7B4900135).
